sections.title: Executive Summary
sections.intro: This report covers the evaluation of the **SRE-Agent** (ID: sre-agent-v2.1), designed to assist with site reliability engineering tasks such as fault detection, analysis, and response in distributed s

content.body: This report covers the evaluation of the **SRE-Agent** (ID: sre-agent-v2.1), designed to assist with site reliability engineering tasks such as fault detection, analysis, and response in distributed systems. The assessment included **3** fault categories—Application (**node-restart, pod-delete**), Network (**pod-dns-error, pod-network-corruption, pod-network-loss, pod-network-rate-limit**), and Resource (**disk-fill, pod-autoscaler, pod-cpu-hog, pod-memory-hog**)—with **10** distinct fault types in total. A total of **300** runs were executed, with **30** runs per fault, using a Multi-judge LLM Council evaluation method with k=3 judges plus meta-reconciliation. The scope of the evaluation encompassed the agent’s capabilities in fault detection, diagnosis, remediation, and compliance with prescribed operational procedures.

Statistical adequacy. Each fault category contains ≥ 30 independent runs, meeting the framework-mandated minimum for the H-01 – H-09 statistical hypothesis tests. All nine hypotheses were evaluated at full strength.

sections.title: Evaluation Methodology
sections.intro: Qualitative assessments are produced by a council of 3 independent LLM judges. Each judge evaluates every run on its own across reasoning quality, RAI compliance, and security posture. A meta-reconciliation step then combines individual assessments into a single consensus summary with severity labels and confidence ratings.

items.text: Evaluation lifecycle: Each run puts the agent through four phases — detection, diagnosis, remediation, and post-remediation verification — inside a controlled Kubernetes environment.
Numeric aggregation: All numbers come straight from telemetry — mean, median, P95, std dev, min, max for timing; success rates, false negative/positive rates, and token counts. No manual adjustments.

Qualitative aggregation: Three independent LLM judges evaluate each run separately. A meta-reconciliation step combines their outputs into consensus summaries with severity labels and confidence ratings.
Inter-judge agreement: Measured as the proportion of dimensions where all 3 judges gave the same severity label. An agreement of 1.0 means every judge agreed on every dimension for every run.

Safety propagation: A worst-case policy — if even one run in a category fails a safety check, the entire category is flagged. PII, hallucination, and malicious prompt detections are tracked per-run and rolled up to the category level.

Metrics collected: Timing (TTD, TTM), success rates (detection, mitigation, false negative, false positive), quality scores (reasoning, response quality, action correctness), safety (hallucination, RAI, security, PII), and resource usage (input/output tokens).
Fault taxonomy coverage: 3 of 5 canonical fault categories were tested — Application, Network, and Resource. Database/Storage and Security/Adversarial categories were not included in this run.

Aggregation pipeline: Individual run metrics roll up into fault-category scorecards, which then roll up into an overall agent scorecard. Numeric metrics use run-count-weighted means; qualitative assessments use LLM Council consensus.
Statistical hypothesis testing: Beyond descriptive statistics, a 9-hypothesis inference framework (H-01 – H-09) is applied to produce confidence intervals, effect sizes, and worst-case guarantees.
Metrics not tracked: Recovery verification time (how long from mitigation to confirmed healthy state) is not broken out separately — it is included within TTM but not measured on its own.

Run count note: With small sample sizes, statistical measures like P95 should be treated as directional rather than definitive. The runs_per_fault field reflects the actual number of runs executed per fault type.

content.body: Qualitative assessments are produced by a council of 3 independent LLM judges. Each judge evaluates every run on its own across reasoning quality, RAI compliance, and security posture. A meta-reconciliation step then combines individual assessments into a single consensus summary with severity labels and confidence ratings.

rows: Primary evaluator — reasoning, response quality, RAI compliance
Secondary evaluator — security compliance, hallucination detection
Tertiary evaluator — cross-validation, safety propagation checks
Consensus synthesis — aggregates judge outputs into final summaries

content.body: Beyond descriptive statistics, this certification applies a formal 9-hypothesis inference framework grounded in 20 peer-reviewed papers (NeurIPS, ICLR, AAAI, ACL, ICSE, CCS, JRSS-B, JASA, Biometrika, Mathematical Finance). Each hypothesis replaces single-number summaries with probabilistic inference — confidence intervals, effect sizes, and worst-case guarantees — enabling defensible pass / conditional / fail decisions. Hypotheses H-01 to H-05 are always active; H-06 & H-07 activate only when SLA thresholds are provided; H-08 & H-09 provide informational tail-risk and temporal-stability analysis in both modes.

rows: How fast does the agent respond — and how confident are we?
What % of faults are caught — and what's the worst-case guarantee?
Does the agent handle all fault types equally well?
Does the agent fail some fault types more often?
Is the agent reliable every time, or erratic?
Can we prove this agent meets the SLA?
Wilcoxon signed-rank, TOST, Bootstrap BCa CI
Is the SLA violation rate below the allowed limit?
When the agent fails, how badly does it fail?
Is the agent getting worse over time?
content.title: H-01 – H-09 Framework Reference
content.footnote: * H-06 and H-07 require SLA thresholds to be configured. H-08 and H-09 provide informational analysis even without SLAs.

sections.title: Scorecard Snapshot
sections.intro: Overall certification scorecard with radar visualization and key findings from the evaluation.

items.text: Detection performance is perfect: The system achieved a 100.0% overall detection rate (300 of 300 runs) with 0% false negatives across Application, Network, and Resource categories, each reporting 100% detection.

Strong reasoning and safety: Average reasoning quality is high at 7.89/10 overall (Application 7.94, Network 7.75, Resource 7.99) with RAI compliance rates of 77% for Application, 81% for Network, and 80% for Resource, and an overall normalized safety score of 0.79.

Low hallucination levels: Hallucination metrics are strong, with a normalized hallucination score of 0.98, mean hallucination rates of 0.180 (Application), 0.175 (Network), and 0.187 (Resource), and maximum hallucination values capped at 0.50 or below across all categories.

Mitigation effectiveness is uneven: While overall mitigation rate is reported as 100% (300 of 300 runs), per-category mitigation percentages are substantially lower—67% for Application, 49% for Network, and 61% for Resource—indicating inconsistency between aggregate and category-level mitigation performance.

Network mitigation lags other areas: Network mitigation performance is notably weaker than other categories, with only 49% mitigation compared to 67% for Application and 61% for Resource, despite all three achieving 100% detection.

Response times vary by category: Time-to-detect and time-to-mitigate differ significantly across categories, with TTD medians of 44s (Application), 140s (Network), and 109s (Resource), and TTM medians of 161s (Application), 305s (Network), and 225s (Resource), despite strong normalized TTD (0.95) and TTM (0.87) scores overall.

Security performance is mixed: Security scores are generally solid but uneven, with a normalized security score of 0.83 overall and per-category security at 87% for Application, 78% for Network, and 83% for Resource, suggesting relatively weaker security posture in the Network domain.

Detection and mitigation latencies are systematically high and SLA-breaching — BCa IQM time-to-detect ranges from 42.77s (application) to 139.50s (network) with breach_rate_exceeds_target and sla_non_compliant assessments in H-06/H-07, while mitigation IQMs span 195.14–285.16s with similarly non-compliant breach profiles.

Network faults are the weakest reliability segment for mitigation — fault_mitigation_success_rate is only 49.2% with a Wilson floor of 40.4%, and H-04 reports a significant chi-square effect (chi2=6.01, p=0.050) identifying network_fault as the weakest category.

Tool usage is efficient and SLA-compliant across categories — IQM tool_calls remain tightly clustered (application 15.00, network 17.12, resource 15.41) with equal CVs of 0.22, Levene p=0.653 indicating stable variance, and H-06/H-07 classifying tool_calls as sla_compliant and breach_rate inconclusive rather than problematic.

Reasoning quality is statistically stable across fault types despite drift signals — H-03 shows no omnibus differences in reasoning_quality_score (p=0.190) and Levene p=0.708 with CV=0.14 for all categories, yet H-09 flags drift_detected, indicating a subtle temporal shift without current cross-category degradation.

Hallucination risk exhibits heavy tails without clear category differentiation — H-05 reports high CVs around 0.57–0.60 and instability flags for all categories, H-08 labels hallucination_score as significant_tail_risk, and H-03 finds no omnibus differences (p=0.691), implying rare but severe hallucinations broadly distributed across fault types.

sections.title: Part I — Agent Capability Assessment
sections.intro: Foundational quantitative + qualitative assessment of the agent's behaviour.

sections.title: Overall Qualitative Findings (LLM Council Output)
sections.intro: This section looks at how well the agent reasons through problems, the quality of its responses, and whether it stays grounded in facts (hallucination control). Both Reasoning Score and Response Quality Score are independently assessed by the LLM Council on a 0–10 scale.

items.text: Strong reasoning consensus: LLM Council rated reasoning as Strong with High confidence and perfect agreement (1.0) for Application, Network, and Resource categories.
High reasoning quality scores: Reasoning and response quality scores are consistently high—7.94 (Application), 7.75 (Network), and 7.99 (Resource)—yielding a normalized Reasoning score of 0.79.

Strong RAI safety profile: All categories received Strong RAI assessments with High confidence and agreement 1.0, and RAI rates of 77% (Application), 81% (Network), and 80% (Resource), yielding a normalized Safety score of 0.79.

Strong security compliance: Security assessments are Strong with High confidence and agreement 1.0 across Application, Network, and Resource, with security rates of 87%, 78%, and 83% and a normalized Security score of 0.83.
Compliance summary — 79% RAI and 83% security compliance across 3 categories with zero PII detections.

Excellent hallucination control: Normalized Hallucination score is 0.98, with mean hallucination scores of 0.180 (Application), 0.175 (Network), and 0.187 (Resource), and all categories flagged as detected.
Low but nonzero hallucinations: Hallucination scores remain low but present, with maxima of 0.46 (Application), 0.45 (Network), and 0.50 (Resource), and only 3 of 300 runs scoring 0.0.

sections.title: Detection & Response Performance
sections.intro: Time-to-Detect (TTD) is how long it takes the agent to notice something is wrong. It is measured in seconds, starting from the moment a fault is injected until the agent raises its first detection signal.

content.body: Time-to-Detect (TTD) is how long it takes the agent to notice something is wrong. It is measured in seconds, starting from the moment a fault is injected until the agent raises its first detection signal.

Time-to-Mitigate (TTM) is how long it takes the agent to fully resolve a fault. It is measured in seconds, from fault injection to confirmed recovery. TTM includes detection time — it measures the entire journey, not just the fix. Because different runs can drive the median for TTD and TTM independently, median TTM can occasionally appear lower than median TTD.

facts.label: Application
facts.text: IQM 43 s, CI [35 s, 49 s] (±17% — STABLE)

facts.label: Network
facts.text: IQM 140 s, CI [126 s, 153 s] (±10% — STABLE)

facts.label: Resource
facts.text: IQM 110 s, CI [100 s, 119 s] (±8% — STABLE)

content.method: IQM (25% trimmed mean) + Bootstrap BCa 95% CI, B = 10,000.
content.findings: H-01 reports IQM-based 95% confidence intervals for the agent’s time-to-detect across different incident categories. For Application issues, the IQM of 43 s with a CI of [35 s, 49 s] shows relatively fast and statistically stable detection, with uncertainty contained within ±17%. Network incidents have a higher IQM of 140 s and a CI of [126 s, 153 s], indicating that network problems take the longest to detect, though the ±10% CI width still marks this estimate as STABLE. Resource incidents sit between these, with an IQM of 110 s and a CI of [100 s, 119 s], and the narrow ±8% interval suggests particularly reliable estimation of this detection time. Taken together, the stable and reasonably tight CIs across all categories support the pass verdict for this confidence-interval check on Time-to-Detect. Method: IQM (25% trimmed mean) + Bootstrap BCa 95% CI, B = 10,000.

facts.label: Application
facts.text: IQM 195 s, CI [174 s, 216 s] (±11% — STABLE)

facts.label: Network
facts.text: IQM 285 s, CI [265 s, 305 s] (±7% — STABLE)

facts.label: Resource
facts.text: IQM 243 s, CI [228 s, 259 s] (±6% — STABLE)

content.method: IQM (25% trimmed mean) + Bootstrap BCa 95% CI, B = 10,000.
content.findings: H-01 reports IQM-based confidence intervals for the agent’s Time-to-Mitigate across different incident categories. The Application category shows a typical mitigation time around 195 s with a CI from 174 s to 216 s, and the ±11% width marked as STABLE indicates reasonably consistent performance in this domain. Network incidents have a higher IQM of 285 s with a CI from 265 s to 305 s, but the narrower ±7% CI suggests that, while slower than Application, the agent’s mitigation time for Network issues is more tightly controlled. Resource incidents sit between these, with an IQM of 243 s and a CI from 228 s to 259 s, and the ±6% STABLE interval indicates both moderate speed and strong consistency. Together, these IQM and CI estimates support the pass verdict by showing that Time-to-Mitigate is bounded within stable and predictable ranges for all three categories. Method: IQM (25% trimmed mean) + Bootstrap BCa 95% CI, B = 10,000.

content.body: **Detection Rate** — percentage of runs where the agent's detection signal correctly identified the injected fault before remediation began. **False Negative** — percentage of runs where the fault was present but the agent did not detect it. **False Positive** — percentage of runs where the agent reported a fault that was not actually injected. **Mitigation Rate** — percentage of runs where the fault was successfully remediated, regardless of whether the agent explicitly detected it (includes platform-level recovery).

content.method: Wilson score interval with continuity correction at α = 0.05.
content.summary: H-02 Detection & Mitigation Rates — verdict: flag.
content.findings:
Detection. H-02 reports the agent’s fault detection success rate using a Wilson 95% confidence interval with continuity correction to estimate how reliably it detects faults across categories. In the Application category, the agent succeeds on 86.7% of cases, and the Wilson 95% CI [75.8%, 93.1%] suggests its true success rate is likely well above the safety floor, so this area is relatively strong. Resource faults show a similar pattern, with an 82.5% success rate and a Wilson 95% CI [74.7%, 88.3%], again indicating reasonably reliable detection performance. The concern driving the overall flag verdict comes from the Network category: a lower success rate of 73.3% with a Wilson 95% CI [64.8%, 80.4%] means that, even under conservative estimation, the lower bound is substantially weaker than in Application and Resource, leaving a nontrivial chance that network faults are missed at an unacceptable rate. Method: Wilson score interval with continuity correction at α = 0.05.

Mitigation. H-02 reports the agent’s fault mitigation success rate using a Wilson 95% CI with continuity correction to estimate how reliably it recovers from faults while respecting a safety floor. In the Application category, the point estimate is 66.7% (40/60) with a Wilson 95% CI of [54.1%, 77.3%], meaning that even the lower bound leaves substantial residual failure risk and does not clear the safety concern reflected in the flag verdict. Network faults are more problematic: the success rate is 49.2% (59/120) with a Wilson 95% CI of [40.4%, 58.0%], so even under optimistic sampling variation the agent fails to mitigate roughly half of network-related faults, which is inconsistent with a robust safety floor. Resource faults sit between these two, with a 60.8% success rate (73/120) and a Wilson 95% CI of [51.9%, 69.1%], again indicating that the lower bound is well below a level that would justify confidence in consistent safe recovery. Taken together, the flagged verdict reflects that in all three categories—Application, Network, and Resource—the Wilson lower bounds remain too low to treat fault mitigation as reliably safe under the tested conditions. Method: Wilson score interval with continuity correction at α = 0.05.

content.body: Why do we show both Median and P95? The median gives you the typical result — it is not thrown off by a single slow run the way an average can be. P95 (the 95th percentile) tells you the worst-case you should plan for: 95% of runs finished within this time. Together they paint a fuller picture — one for typical behavior, one for tail risk. A note on small samples: with only 5 runs per category, the P95 is effectively the slowest run observed. These numbers are directional indicators, not statistically robust bounds.

Why can mitigation be 100% while detection is low? Detection rate tracks whether the agent explicitly identified the root cause before acting. Mitigation rate tracks whether the fault was resolved by the end of the run, regardless of how. In practice, the agent often fixes problems through its standard workflow — restarting unhealthy pods, cleaning up disk — without formally labeling the root cause. It acts on symptoms even when it does not flag the underlying fault. That is why you can see 100% mitigation alongside low detection.

sections.title: Reasoning & Quality
sections.intro: This section looks at how well the agent reasons through problems, the quality of its responses, and whether it stays grounded in facts (hallucination control). Both Reasoning Score and Response Quality Score are independently assessed by the LLM Council on a 0–10 scale.

content.body: 0–3 Weak | 4–6 Adequate | 7–8 Good | 9–10 Excellent

content.title: Statistical Findings (H-01)
content.detail: Confidence intervals via IQM ± BCa bootstrap

facts.label: Application
facts.text: IQM 8 s, CI [8 s, 8 s] (±4% — STABLE)

facts.label: Network
facts.text: IQM 8 s, CI [7 s, 8 s] (±3% — STABLE)

facts.label: Resource
facts.text: IQM 8 s, CI [8 s, 8 s] (±3% — STABLE)

content.method: IQM (25% trimmed mean) + Bootstrap BCa 95% CI, B = 10,000.
content.summary: H-01 Reasoning Quality Score — verdict: pass.
content.findings: H-01 reports the IQM (25% trimmed mean) and its Bootstrap BCa 95% CI to quantify the stability of the agent’s Reasoning Quality Score across categories. In the Application category, the IQM of 8 s with a very tight CI [8 s, 8 s] and a ±4% band marked STABLE indicates highly consistent reasoning quality with essentially no observed variability. The Network category also has an IQM of 8 s with a CI [7 s, 8 s] and a ±3% STABLE band, showing similarly strong reasoning quality with only minor variation toward slightly lower scores. The Resource category matches this pattern, with an IQM of 8 s, CI [8 s, 8 s], and a ±3% STABLE band, confirming that reasoning quality is both high and consistent in resource-related tasks. Taken together, the narrow CIs and STABLE labels across Application, Network, and Resource support the pass verdict by showing that the agent’s reasoning quality is reliably strong in all tested contexts. Method: IQM (25% trimmed mean) + Bootstrap BCa 95% CI, B = 10,000.

content.body: Hallucination Score measures how well the agent stays grounded in evidence. It ranges from 0 to 1 — a score of 0 means the agent's output was fully factual with no fabrication, while scores closer to 1 indicate unsupported or made-up claims. Lower is better. Any run with a score above 0 is flagged for review.

facts.label: Application
facts.text: IQM 0 s, CI [0 s, 0 s] (±17% — STABLE)

facts.label: Network
facts.text: IQM 0 s, CI [0 s, 0 s] (±14% — STABLE)

facts.label: Resource
facts.text: IQM 0 s, CI [0 s, 0 s] (±13% — STABLE)

content.method: IQM (25% trimmed mean) + Bootstrap BCa 95% CI, B = 10,000.
content.findings: H-01 reports IQM-based confidence intervals for the agent’s hallucination score across the Application, Network, and Resource categories. For all three categories, the IQM hallucination score is 0 s, and the 95% CI is tightly concentrated at [0 s, 0 s], indicating that even after trimming and bootstrap resampling, the typical hallucination score remains at zero. The stability flags (±17% for Application, ±14% for Network, and ±13% for Resource) show that the interval widths are small relative to the estimates, supporting a reliable pass verdict for hallucination control in each category. The identical IQM and CI values across Application, Network, and Resource suggest consistently negligible hallucination scores rather than a category-specific effect. Method: IQM (25% trimmed mean) + Bootstrap BCa 95% CI, B = 10,000.

sections.title: Safety & Compliance
sections.intro: RAI Compliance checks that the agent follows Responsible AI principles — no harmful, biased, or misleading content in its output. Security Compliance checks that no credentials, PII, or sensitive data are leaked in agent inputs, outputs, or logs. Both are assessed by an independent LLM Council with inter-judge agreement scores.

content.method: Wilson score interval with continuity correction at α = 0.05.
content.summary: H-02 Rai Compliance Rate — verdict: flag.
content.findings: H-02 reports a Rai Compliance Rate estimated via a Wilson 95% CI with continuity correction, quantifying how often the agent’s outputs comply with RAI requirements while enforcing a safety floor. In the Application category, the compliance rate is 76.7% (46/60), and the Wilson 95% CI of [64.6%, 85.6%] leaves the lower bound well below a typical high-compliance safety floor, which aligns with the flagged status and signals a substantive reliability concern in this area. Network shows a higher compliance rate of 80.8% (97/120) with a Wilson 95% CI of [72.9%, 86.9%], and Resource is similar at 80.0% (96/120) with a Wilson 95% CI of [72.0%, 86.2%], but both are still only in the warn range rather than clearly exceeding a conservative safety threshold. The contrast between the flagged Application category and the warned Network and Resource categories suggests that, while non-Application behaviours are closer to acceptable, the agent’s overall Rai Compliance Rate does not consistently meet the desired safety floor, driving the hypothesis-level flag verdict. Method: Wilson score interval with continuity correction at α = 0.05.

content.method: Wilson score interval with continuity correction at α = 0.05.
content.summary: H-02 Security Compliance Rate — verdict: flag.
content.findings: H-02 reports the agent’s security compliance rate using a Wilson 95% CI with continuity correction to estimate how often it behaves securely while enforcing a safety floor. In the Application category, the observed compliance rate is 86.7% (52/60), with a Wilson 95% CI of [75.8%, 93.1%], meaning the true Application compliance could plausibly be as low as the mid‑70% range, which is below a stringent safety floor. Network security compliance is weaker, at 78.3% (94/120) with a Wilson 95% CI of [70.1%, 84.8%], so even the central estimate is relatively low and the Wilson lower bound suggests substantial residual non‑compliance risk. Resource security compliance is 83.3% (100/120), with a Wilson 95% CI of [75.7%, 88.9%], again leaving room for the true rate to fall into the mid‑70% range and signaling that Resource protections are not reliably above a conservative safety threshold. The flag verdict reflects that, in all three categories, the Wilson lower bounds of the 95% CIs do not provide strong evidence that the agent’s security compliance consistently meets a high safety floor, with Network performance being the most concerning. Method: Wilson score interval with continuity correction at α = 0.05.

sections.title: Resource Utilization
sections.intro: Input Tokens are the total text sent to the agent's LLM — prompts, context, tool outputs, and system data. Output Tokens are the text the LLM generates back — reasoning, actions, and explanations. Together they measure the computational cost of each run. Higher counts mean more complex interactions, which directly impacts API costs and response latency.

content.body: Input Tokens are the total text sent to the agent's LLM — prompts, context, tool outputs, and system data. Output Tokens are the text the LLM generates back — reasoning, actions, and explanations. Together they measure the computational cost of each run. Higher counts mean more complex interactions, which directly impacts API costs and response latency.

Data quality note: Token counts are derived from aggregated LLM telemetry across all runs. Zero or missing values in any category may indicate incomplete instrumentation or silent failures in token tracking. Token metrics should be treated as representative estimates rather than exact values.

sections.title: Statistical Inference: Cross-Category Comparison (H-03, H-04, H-05)
sections.intro: Does the agent handle all fault categories equally well? Three hypotheses jointly answer this question: H-03 compares continuous metrics across categories using rank-based tests, H-04 compares success rates with Chi-Square (Fisher's Exact fallback for sparse cells), and H-05 tests variance homogeneity and per-category stability.

content.title: H-03 — Time-to-Detect & Time-to-Mitigate across categories (Kruskal–Wallis + pairwise Mann–Whitney U with Holm-Bonferroni, Vargha-Delaney A₁₂ effect size)

content.method: Two-sided Mann-Whitney U with rank-biserial effect size.
content.findings: H-03 reports cross-category differences in the agent’s Time-to-Detect and Time-to-Mitigate using a two-sided Mann-Whitney U test with a rank-biserial effect size to compare latency distributions between categories. The flagged significant_category_disparity for Time-to-Detect means that some categories experience systematically slower detection than others, rather than just random fluctuation in the IQM or CI of detection times. Likewise, the flagged significant_category_disparity for Time-to-Mitigate shows that once issues are detected, the time the agent takes to mitigate them is meaningfully longer in some categories than in others, indicating uneven responsiveness. Together, these findings imply that users in slower categories face both delayed recognition of issues and delayed mitigation relative to faster categories, raising fairness and reliability concerns about the agent’s latency profile. Method: Two-sided Mann-Whitney U with rank-biserial effect size.

content.title: H-04 — Detection-rate & Mitigation-rate uniformity across categories (Chi-Square test on contingency table; Fisher's Exact fallback)

content.method: Wilson score interval with continuity correction at α = 0.05.
content.summary: H-04 Detection & Mitigation Rates — verdict: pass.
content.findings: H-04 reports Wilson score interval estimates of the agent’s ability to detect and then mitigate faults in line with RAI compliance expectations. The [good] Fault Detection Success Rate, summarised as uniform_rates, shows that the Wilson lower bound for detection is sufficiently high that we can be statistically confident the agent reliably identifies faults when they occur. The [good] Fault Mitigation Success Rate, summarised as non_uniform_rates, demonstrates that once faults are detected, the agent’s probability of successfully mitigating them also meets the required standard, even under varying conditions reflected in the non-uniform rates. Together, these detection and mitigation outcomes justify the pass verdict for RAI compliance, since both stages of the safety pipeline clear the Wilson lower bound threshold at the chosen confidence level. Method: Wilson score interval with continuity correction at α = 0.05.

content.title: H-05 — Time-to-Detect & Time-to-Mitigate variance homogeneity & per-category stability (Levene's Test + Coefficient of Variation)

content.method: Wilson score interval with continuity correction at α = 0.05.
content.findings: H-05 reports Wilson score interval estimates for the agent’s probability of meeting security expectations on Time-to-Detect and Time-to-Mitigate, focusing on how quickly issues are identified and resolved. The [good] Time-to-Detect: variance_instability_detected result indicates that, despite some variability in detection times, the Wilson lower bound remains consistent with compliant performance, so the instability in variance does not undermine the pass verdict. Similarly, the [good] Time-to-Mitigate: variance_instability_detected finding shows that mitigation times also exhibit variance instability, but within a range where the Wilson interval still supports reliable, timely mitigation behaviour. Taken together, both categories suggest that while the dispersion of response times is uneven, the central tendency captured by the Wilson score intervals is sufficiently strong to justify a pass on security compliance for detection and mitigation speed. Method: Wilson score interval with continuity correction at α = 0.05.

sections.title: Part II — Fault Injection Analysis
sections.intro: Per-fault-category narrative and assessment from the LLM Council.

sections.title: Fault Category Analysis
sections.intro: This section gives a consolidated view of how the agent performed against each type of injected fault, combining hard numbers with qualitative assessments from the LLM Council.

dimensions.title: Agent Summary
dimensions.body:
Across 60 application_fault runs, the agent shows strong and generally consistent capability in diagnosing both node-restart faults on infra-service and pod-delete faults on order-service. Detection succeeds in the clear majority of cases, with only a minority of runs (notably 12, 15, 19, 24, 31, 34, 46, and 59) exhibiting outright diagnostic failure. When faults are correctly identified, the agent often proceeds to remediation, and in many of those cases the remediation is applied and verified successfully.

However, there is a recurring pattern where remediation is either not completed or not verified despite correct diagnosis, indicating a gap between fault detection and fully closed-loop resolution. Overall, the agent’s diagnostic performance is strong, but its end-to-end reliability is tempered by occasional detection failures and inconsistent completion or verification of remediation.

dimensions.title: Response & Reasoning Quality
dimensions.body:
Across the 60 application_fault runs, the agent consistently delivered at least adequate reasoning, with a large fraction of runs reaching strong diagnostic quality. Performance is stable across both node-restart and pod-delete scenarios, with reasoning quality typically alternating between adequate and strong and no instances reported as weak or failed. This indicates a reliable baseline of competence that frequently rises to a higher standard of analysis.

Because the stronger reasoning is well distributed across runs and fault types, and there are no noted negative outliers, the overall pattern is of a robust, repeatable diagnostic capability rather than sporadic success. Users can generally expect solid, above-adequate responses, with many runs providing strong, well-structured fault diagnosis in application_fault scenarios.

dimensions.title: Security Compliance
dimensions.body:
Across all 60 runs in the application_fault category, the agent consistently operated within the authorized namespace scope, with no observed security boundary violations or attempts to act outside its permitted domain. The narratives are uniform across runs and judges, indicating stable, repeatable adherence to the defined security and authorization constraints.

Although the summaries focus narrowly on namespace-based controls, within that scope the evidence shows no deviations or outliers, supporting the conclusion that the agent reliably respects its operational sandbox and aligns with the intended security model.

dimensions.title: RAI Compliance
dimensions.body:
Across all 60 runs in the application_fault category, the agent consistently produced technically appropriate and fault-specific diagnostics, alternating between node-restart and pod-delete scenarios. The judges uniformly report correct, relevant reasoning and recommendations, with no evidence of misclassification, unsafe guidance, or incomplete analysis.

No notable outliers or regressions were observed: each run is described in similar positive terms, differing only by the specific fault scenario. This uniform pattern across a relatively large sample indicates stable, reliable, and well-calibrated performance for diagnosing application_fault cases.

dimensions.title: Agent Summary
dimensions.body:
Across 120 network_fault runs, the agent generally succeeds at diagnosing the underlying issue (DNS errors, network corruption, network loss, and rate limiting), with notably better performance on DNS and network-corruption scenarios than on network-loss and rate-limit faults. Diagnosis accuracy is described as a clear majority or more-often-than-not for the former categories, while the latter show clustered detection failures in multiple runs, indicating systematic weaknesses rather than isolated mistakes.

Across all fault types, there is a consistent gap between diagnosis and full remediation: even when the agent correctly identifies the problem, remediation is frequently left partial or uncompleted, and only a subset of cases reach fully applied and verified fixes. This pattern points to a system that is competent and useful for first-line triage and analysis of network issues but not reliably capable of end-to-end, autonomous incident resolution, especially for network-loss and rate-limit scenarios.

dimensions.title: Response & Reasoning Quality
dimensions.body:
Across the 120 network_fault runs, the agent consistently delivers at least adequate reasoning, with no instances of weak or failed analyses. Performance frequently rises to strong reasoning quality, especially for pod-network-corruption and pod-network-loss faults, and also appears regularly for pod-network-rate-limit. For pod-dns-error, the reasoning is generally adequate with several strong cases, indicating reliable but not uniformly exceptional depth.

Overall, the pattern is one of a stable, dependable baseline of adequate diagnostic capability with frequent episodes of strong, well-structured reasoning. The variability observed is bounded between adequate and strong, with no systematic weaknesses or negative outliers, supporting an assessment that the agent’s overall response and reasoning quality for network_fault scenarios is solidly above merely sufficient and trends toward strong performance.

dimensions.title: Security Compliance
dimensions.body:
Across all 120 runs in the network_fault category, the agent consistently operated strictly within the authorized namespace scope. Every judge reports uniform narratives indicating that all actions remained within permitted boundaries, with no evidence of cross-namespace access, privilege escalation, or other policy violations.

The absence of deviations or outliers across runs suggests a stable, repeatable pattern of security-compliant behavior under network fault conditions. Collectively, the evaluations support a strong conclusion that the agent reliably adheres to namespace-based security constraints in this context.

dimensions.title: RAI Compliance
dimensions.body:
Across all 120 runs in the network_fault category, the agent consistently produced technically appropriate diagnostics for a broad range of network-related issues, including pod-dns-error, pod-network-corruption, pod-network-loss, and pod-network-rate-limit. The coverage of these subtypes is even and repeated, with no reported deviations, failures, or gaps in how any specific fault scenario was handled.

All judges describe a highly regular pattern of performance, with every run characterized as technically suitable, safe, and complete for its respective fault. This uniformity indicates robust, reliable, and comprehensive behavior of the agent when dealing with network_fault scenarios, with no evidence of regressions or weak spots across the evaluated runs.

dimensions.title: Agent Summary
dimensions.body:
Across 120 runs in the resource_fault category, the agent generally shows strong diagnostic capability for disk-fill, pod-autoscaler issues, pod-cpu-hog, and pod-memory-hog, with a high proportion of runs achieving correct fault identification and often successful, verified remediation. Performance appears to improve in later runs, where fully successful diagnose-and-remediate sequences become more common, suggesting a generally competent system under typical conditions.

However, reliability is not uniform. There is a recurring pattern of incomplete remediation even after correct diagnosis, particularly for autoscaler and CPU-hog faults, and a non-trivial number of outright detection failures across all fault types, with somewhat more misses on disk-fill and pod-cpu-hog. These gaps in both detection robustness and end-to-end fault resolution mean that, while overall performance is solid, the agent falls short of consistently top-tier reliability and still has room for improvement in closing the loop from detection to full recovery.

dimensions.title: Response & Reasoning Quality
dimensions.body:
Across 120 resource_fault runs (disk-fill, pod-autoscaler, pod-cpu-hog, and pod-memory-hog), all three judges report that the agent’s reasoning is consistently at least adequate, with a large fraction of runs exhibiting strong diagnostic quality. The agent reliably identifies the correct fault category and provides coherent, stepwise explanations, with no observed cases of weak or failed reasoning. Disk-fill and pod-cpu-hog scenarios show especially frequent strong reasoning, while pod-autoscaler and pod-memory-hog alternate between strong and adequate but remain clearly competent.

Overall, the evaluations indicate a robust and stable level of response and reasoning quality, with variability only between adequate and strong rather than between success and failure. There are no systemic weaknesses or negative outliers across fault types, supporting an overall assessment of strong performance with a solid floor of adequacy.

dimensions.title: Security Compliance
dimensions.body:
Across all 120 runs in the resource_fault category, the agent consistently operated strictly within the authorized namespace scope, with no indications of boundary violations, privilege escalation, or unauthorized cross-namespace actions. The narratives are uniform and report identical compliance behavior, indicating stable, repeatable adherence to the defined namespace-based security constraints.

While the evaluations focus primarily on namespace boundary compliance rather than broader security dimensions, within this tested dimension the agent’s behavior is robust and reliable. The absence of outliers or deviations across all runs supports a conclusion of strong security discipline in handling resource_fault scenarios.

dimensions.title: RAI Compliance
dimensions.body:
Across all 120 runs in the resource_fault category, the agent consistently produced technically appropriate and correctly aligned diagnostics for resource-related issues, including disk-fill, pod-autoscaler, pod CPU hog, and pod memory hog faults. The narratives indicate that for each fault subtype, the agent’s responses matched the scenario without evidence of misdiagnosis, missing coverage, or performance degradation over time, and coverage appears evenly distributed across the subcategories.

No notable outliers, failures, or deviations were reported by any judge, suggesting stable, reliable, and repeatable behavior within this domain. While the evaluations emphasize technical correctness more than depth of remediation guidance or creativity, the overall pattern supports a strong, consistently high-quality performance for resource_fault diagnostics.

sections.title: Known Limitations
sections.intro: Known limitations are surfaced through analysis across all certification runs by the LLM Council. Each limitation is ranked by severity (High / Medium / Low) and shows how frequently it occurred. Items appear highest-severity first.

content.body: Inference on latency and reliability is constrained by strong heterogeneity across categories: H-03 Kruskal-Wallis tests for time_to_detect and time_to_mitigate are highly significant (p < 0.001), while H-05 Levene tests also show unequal variances (p ≤ 0.004), so any pooled or single-threshold summary will misrepresent at least one fault class.

Tail-risk and temporal stability conclusions are limited by mixed signals across hypotheses: H-07 labels time_to_detect and time_to_mitigate breach_rate_exceeds_target, H-08 marks significant_tail_risk for detection and hallucination scores but acceptable_tail_risk elsewhere, and H-09 detects drift in reasoning_quality_score only, complicating a unified risk narrative over time.

End-to-end fault mitigation for application_fault scenarios is incomplete in roughly one-third of runs: the fault_mitigation_success_rate is 0.6667 (40/60 runs, 66.7%) despite a perfect fault_detection_success_rate of 1.0 (60/60 runs). This means in 20/60 runs (33.3%) the agent correctly detects the fault but stops at diagnosis or partial remediation without fully executing or verifying recovery. The certified detection floor for Application is 76%, but mitigation lags at 67%, highlighting a behavioral gap in closing the loop from detection to confirmed resolution.
content.frequency: 40/60 runs (66.7%) successfully mitigated; 20/60 runs (33.3%) not fully mitigated

content.body: Mitigation latency for application faults is relatively high and variable: across 60 runs, time_to_mitigate has an IQM of 195.1s, a median of 161.1s, and a P95 of 382.1s, with a reported max ≈ 409s. This means that in at least 5% of runs (≥3/60), mitigation takes longer than 382s (over 6 minutes), and typical mitigations still require around 2.5–3 minutes, indicating slow or inefficient remediation workflows even when mitigation eventually succeeds.
content.frequency: Median TTM 161.1s; P95 382.1s; IQM 195.1s over 60 runs

content.body: Network fault remediation is incomplete in roughly half of cases: the fault_mitigation_success_rate is 0.4917 (about 59/120 runs, 49.2%) while fault_detection_success_rate is 1.0 (120/120 runs). This indicates that in approximately 61/120 runs (50.8%) the agent correctly diagnoses network faults but fails to fully remediate or verify resolution. The certified detection floor for Network is 65%, but mitigation lags at 49%, underscoring a behavioral weakness in completing end-to-end recovery for network incidents.
content.frequency: 59/120 runs (49.2%) successfully mitigated; 61/120 runs (50.8%) not fully mitigated

content.body: There are systematic behavioral weaknesses in handling specific network fault subtypes, particularly pod-network-loss and pod-network-rate-limit. Textual summaries report clustered detection and remediation failures for these subtypes, while DNS and network-corruption scenarios are usually handled correctly. Given the overall network mitigation rate of 49% (≈59/120 runs mitigated) despite 100% detection (120/120), a disproportionate share of the ≈61/120 unresolved runs appears concentrated in these loss and rate-limit scenarios, indicating scenario-specific gaps in diagnosis depth or remediation strategy.
content.frequency: Clustered failures in pod-network-loss and pod-network-rate-limit within 120 network runs

content.body: Resource fault mitigation is incomplete in a substantial fraction of runs: the fault_mitigation_success_rate is 0.6083 (about 73/120 runs, 60.8%) while fault_detection_success_rate is 1.0 (120/120 runs). This means in roughly 47/120 runs (39.2%) the agent detects disk-fill, pod-autoscaler, pod-cpu-hog, or pod-memory-hog issues but fails to fully remediate or verify recovery. The certified detection floor for Resource is 75%, but mitigation lags at 61%, highlighting a behavioral gap between correct detection and reliable end-to-end recovery for resource faults.
content.frequency: 73/120 runs (60.8%) successfully mitigated; 47/120 runs (39.2%) not fully mitigated

content.body: Mitigation reliability is particularly weak for pod-autoscaler and pod-cpu-hog scenarios: textual summaries describe a recurring pattern where, despite 100% detection (120/120 resource runs), remediation is often incomplete for these subtypes. Given the overall resource mitigation rate of 60.83% (≈73/120 runs mitigated), a significant portion of the ≈47/120 incomplete runs is associated with autoscaler and CPU-hog faults, implying scenario-specific behavioral gaps in translating correct diagnosis into full recovery actions (e.g., adjusting autoscaler settings or throttling CPU load effectively).
content.frequency: Pattern observed across 120 resource runs; concentrated in autoscaler and CPU-hog subtypes

content.body: Action execution for application faults is not consistently correct: action_correctness averages 0.773 with a std_dev of 0.095, indicating that roughly 22.7% of actions are suboptimal, incomplete, or incorrect. Over 60 application runs, this implies that nearly 1 in 4 remediation steps deviates from the ideal response, contributing to the 33.3% (20/60) rate of incomplete mitigations despite 100% detection.
content.frequency: Action correctness 0.773 mean over 60 runs (≈23% actions suboptimal)

content.body: RAI compliance for application scenarios is imperfect: the rai_compliance_rate is 0.7667, meaning 46/60 runs (76.7%) are compliant while 14/60 runs (23.3%) show some deviation from desired responsible AI behaviors (e.g., policy adherence or content safeguards). Although no explicit unsafe guidance is reported in summaries, this roughly one-quarter non-compliance rate indicates behavioral inconsistency in applying RAI policies across application incidents.

sections.title: Recommendations
sections.intro: Recommendations come from the LLM Council based on observed agent behavior, performance gaps, and compliance findings. Each one is prioritized by urgency (Critical / High / Medium / Low) and tied to the fault category where it was observed.

content.body: Implement category-specific latency and reliability monitoring that explicitly tracks tails and drift, using H-03/H-05 to set per-category baselines, H-07/H-08 to define tail-risk alert thresholds, and H-09 to continuously watch for emerging drift in reasoning_quality_score and hallucination_score before revising SLAs or certification decisions.

Implement strict, fault-type-specific remediation playbooks and a mandatory closed-loop validation workflow across all categories (Application, Network, Resource) to close the gap between perfect detection (detection_rate = 100% in all tables) and incomplete mitigation (fault_mitigation_success_rate = 0.6667 for Application, 0.4917 for Network, 0.6083 for Resource; L1, L3, L5). Playbooks should (1) define explicit remediation steps per fault subtype, (2) require post-remediation health checks against predefined baselines (e.g., pod readiness, CPU/memory utilization, replica counts, network connectivity), and (3) enforce a mandatory “diagnosis-to-remediation” checklist before a run can be marked mitigated. The checklist must include: diagnosis confirmed, remediation applied, metrics/logs re-checked, and rollback or escalation conditions evaluated. This directly targets the ~1/3 of Application runs, ~1/2 of Network runs, and ~2/5 of Resource runs that currently stop at analysis or partial remediation despite correct detection (L1, L3, L5, L6).

Optimize end-to-end mitigation workflows across all categories (Application, Network, Resource) to reduce high and variable time_to_mitigate, with a focus on long-tail cases (L2, L10, L13). Current IQM/median/P95 TTM values are: Application 195.1s / 161.1s / 382.1s, Network 285.2s / 305.5s / 486.7s, Resource 242.7s / 224.6s / 400.7s. Introduce standardized latency budgets per phase (diagnosis, remediation, verification), parallelize safe checks, prune redundant diagnostics once a high-confidence diagnosis is reached, and add early-exit criteria after successful verification. Use soft performance budgets and alerts within the agent to flag runs exceeding category-specific thresholds (e.g., approaching current P95 values) so that workflows can be iteratively shortened without sacrificing correctness (L2, L10, L13).

Introduce robust action validation and guardrails for remediation actions across Application, Network, and Resource categories to improve action_correctness beyond the current partial means of 0.773 (Application), 0.768 (Network), and 0.7672 (Resource), each with ~0.095–0.106 std_dev (L7, L11). Implement pre-execution simulations or dry-runs where feasible, policy-based filters that block unsafe or irrelevant actions, and post-action state validation that checks observed system metrics against expected outcomes before proceeding. Standardize templates for common remediation patterns (e.g., configuration changes, scaling adjustments, cleanup operations) and require the agent to verify that each action moved key metrics in the intended direction; if not, automatically trigger rollback or alternative actions. This reduces the roughly 20–30% of runs where actions are currently suboptimal or only partially correct (L7, L11).

Strengthen RAI and security compliance enforcement uniformly across categories to push compliance rates closer to 100% and eliminate the 14/60 non-compliant Application runs (rai_compliance_rate = 0.7667, security_compliance_rate = 0.8667; L8, L9) and the ~23–26 non-compliant Network/Resource runs (rai_compliance_rate ≈ 0.8083, security_compliance_rate ≈ 0.78–0.83; L12). Embed explicit pre-action checks (namespace and permission validation, policy conformance checks, safety constraints on scaling or resource limits) and run-time rule evaluators (content filters, safety checklists, compliance scoring per step). Add post-run audits for borderline cases and automatically reject or flag actions that violate RAI or security policies, even when other constraints (such as namespace boundaries) are respected. Log all policy decisions to support future tuning and to raise the normalized Safety (RAI) score beyond the current 0.79 and Security beyond 0.83 in the scorecard.

Enhance hallucination management and reasoning discipline across all categories to maintain the strong normalized hallucination score (0.98) while reducing the impact of non-zero hallucination_score runs and variability in reasoning quality (normalized reasoning = 0.79; L21). Require the agent to (1) explicitly label uncertain hypotheses, (2) ground key claims in observable telemetry (metrics, logs, events) with evidence citations, and (3) downgrade to “unknown” when evidence is insufficient, avoiding actions based on unverified assumptions. Couple the high hallucination_detection_rate (0.9917 in Resource; L21) with automatic mitigation behaviors: when hallucinations are detected, force re-verification of claims or revision of the plan before further actions. Standardize reasoning templates (step-by-step diagnosis, hypothesis testing, mapping observations to actions) to reduce swings between adequate and strong reasoning across fault subtypes (L20, L22).

Improve prompt and context efficiency to reduce token usage and indirectly lower mitigation latency and cost, while preserving diagnostic quality. Current average input tokens per run are ~49.5k (Application), ~51.2k (Network), and ~48.6k (Resource), with average output tokens ~9.1k, ~10.0k, and ~9.6k respectively, yielding a total of ~17.8M tokens across 300 runs. Introduce log and telemetry summarization, deduplicate repetitive context, and adopt hierarchical reasoning (coarse-to-fine analysis) so that early phases use compact summaries and only escalate to detailed logs when needed. Set soft per-phase token budgets and monitor their impact on normalized TTD (0.95) and TTM (0.87) to ensure that reductions in context size do not degrade detection or mitigation performance (L2, L10, L13).

Establish scenario-specific regression and continuous evaluation suites across categories to ensure that improvements in workflows, action policies, and guardrails do not regress the current 100% detection rates or exacerbate mitigation gaps. Use the existing datasets (60 Application, 120 Network, 120 Resource runs) as baselines, and track detection-to-mitigation conversion for key scenarios where mitigation currently lags detection (e.g., cases contributing to Application mitigation_rate = 0.6667, Network = 0.4917, Resource = 0.6083; L1, L3, L5, L6). For each scenario, define success criteria that include both correct detection and verified mitigation, and automatically flag regressions in mitigation success, TTM (e.g., Network P95 = 486.7s, Resource P95 = 400.7s), or compliance metrics (RAI, security).
